title: Geometrical derivation of Wigner's angle for arbitrary Lorentz transformations of massless particles
abstract: This note summarizes the physics and mathematics of Lorentz transformations for massless particles, specifically for photons. We provide a complete analytical derivation of Wigner's little group matrix and a closed formula for the calculation of Wigner's angle for arbitrary Lorentz transformations. Our derivation highlights the geometrical content of the sequence of little group transformations leading to Wigner's matrix and links it to classical theorems in spherical trigonometry.
summary: This paper provides a new geometric derivation for the Wigner angle associated with Lorentz transformations of massless particles like photons. By treating the transformations as paths on a unit sphere, the authors derive an explicit formula for the angle and connect it to the topological Berry phase. This clarifies the relationship between relativistic kinematics and geometric phases.
main_problem: Deriving a closed-form analytical expression for Wigner's angle for arbitrary Lorentz transformations of massless particles and addressing the non-uniqueness of this angle.
main_result: The authors provide a complete analytical derivation of the Wigner matrix and a closed formula for the Wigner angle, linking it to spherical trigonometry and the topological Berry phase.
method: A geometric approach using quaternion algebra, spherical trigonometry (including the spherical law of cosines and Gauss-Bonnet theorem), and the polar decomposition of Lorentz transformations.
model_or_system: Massless particles, specifically photons, within the framework of Special Relativity and the unitary irreducible representations of the Poincaré group.
key_observables: Wigner angle (phi_W), Wigner matrix (W), helicity (sigma), and Wigner rotation axis.
important_parameters: Rotation axes (n_i), rotation angles (phi_i), relative velocities (v), and helicity (sigma).
main_assumptions: The work is confined to the framework of Special Relativity and does not account for gravitational effects; the initial 4-momentum is assumed to be in a standard form.
figures_summary: Figure 1a shows the non-uniqueness of rotation axes; Figure 1b illustrates the spherical and polar triangles in momentum space; Figure 2 shows the Wigner angle as a function of the angle between momentum vectors; Figure 3 compares the Wigner angle to Thomas precession; Figure 4 shows the Wigner angle varying with a rotation parameter theta.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of Wigner's angle, provides a geometric derivation using quaternions and spherical trigonometry, presents closed-form analytical results, and concludes with numerical validation and comparisons to known effects.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Representation of the rotations on a unit sphere
FIG. 2: Wigner angle (φW in radian) as a function of the angle (φˆz−→ˆp) between ˆz and ˆp = [sin(φˆz−→ˆp), 0, −cos(φˆz−→ˆp)], for vˆz = 2/3c and vˆb = 8 km/s.
FIG. 4: Wigner angle (φW in radian) for the family of rotations parametrized by the angle θ, in radian, for ˆp = [sin(π/4), 0, −cos(π/4)], ˆb = [cos(π/4), sin(π/4), 0], vˆz = 2/3c and vˆb = 8 km/s.
FIG. 3: Wigner angle (φW ) for varying values of vˆb, for ˆp = [sin(π/4), 0, −cos(π/4)], ˆb = [cos(π/4), sin(π/4), 0] and vˆz = 2/3c.

Fig. 4 displays the Wigner angle for the family of ro- tations (taking ˆz to ˆp) parameterized by the angle θ, as deﬁned in Eq. (20). The Wigner angle oscillates with θ, although in a limited range. These variations indi- cate that for a given ˆp and boost Λ, the Wigner angle is not unique but depends on the rotation of the standard Lorentz transformation L(p).
